title: Throwing a stoneware oval dish
subtitle: A round thrown dish reshaped to an oval at leather-hard, glazed and fired to cone 6
excerpt: Throw a standard round shallow dish, then at leather-hard gently press the rim at two opposing points to convert it to an oval. Low sides approximately 40 mm, wide base. Glazed and fired to cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Requires a potter's wheel and kiln.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage at which clay holds its shape but remains damp enough to trim and join
Fully air-dried unfired clay; fragile before bisque firing
The first kiln firing (cone 06, approx 1000 degrees C) that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware
The trimmed-back base ridge a finished pot sits on
Pyrometric measure of heat-work in a kiln. Cone 06 approx 1000 degrees C, cone 6 approx 1220 degrees C, cone 10 approx 1280 degrees C

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no dry sanding indoors.

Ensure adequate ventilation when firing: a downdraft vent or open window with an extractor. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach.
An oval dish is produced not by throwing an oval directly on the wheel (which is impossible) but by throwing a standard round dish and reshaping it at
leather-hard

. The window for reshaping is narrow: the clay must be firm enough to hold its new shape without springing back to round, but not so firm that pressing the rim causes cracking. The finished dish fires to
cone
6 (1220 degrees C) and works as a baking dish, serving dish, or soap dish.

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: as needed for dipping or brushing

content.text: Preparation

Wedge 600 g to 700 g of smooth stoneware. Attach a bat to the wheel head before throwing: a wide flat dish removed directly from the wheel head without a bat almost always distorts. The round thrown form will be left on the bat throughout drying until the reshaping step.
Throwing the round dish
Centre the clay and flatten it into a low, wide form. Open the floor outward to approximately 150 mm, leaving a floor 9 to 10 mm thick. Compress the floor thoroughly with the wooden rib.

Pull up low walls approximately 40 mm tall, keeping them at 8 mm thickness at the base. The final dish diameter should be approximately 200 to 220 mm. Flare the rim outward very slightly: this helps with reshaping later and gives a natural lip to the finished oval dish.
Compress the rim with the chamois leather. Smooth the exterior wall with the wooden rib. Check the floor thickness with the needle tool: 9 to 10 mm.

Leave the round dish on the bat. Cover loosely with thin plastic to slow the drying and equalise moisture across the floor and walls. This prevents the rim drying faster than the floor, which causes warping.
Reshaping to oval at leather-hard

When the dish has dried to firm leather-hard (the rim holds its shape when pressed lightly but does not crack), it is ready to reshape. Test by pressing the rim gently inward at one point: the clay should move and stay without cracking or springing back. If it springs back, leave it for another hour. If it cracks at the slightest pressure, it has dried too far past the
leather-hard
window.

Identify two opposing points on the rim approximately 90 degrees from the intended long axis of the oval. With both hands, apply gentle but steady inward pressure at these two opposing points simultaneously, pressing the rim about 15 to 20 mm inward on each side. The floor will bow slightly upward in the centre as the diameter shortens: this is normal.
Check that the two long sides of the now-oval are symmetrical when viewed from above. Adjust by pressing the longer side inward slightly.

Place the oval dish on a flat surface and check it sits level without rocking. If the floor has lifted unevenly, press the high corner down gently. Allow the oval form to stiffen in the new shape for at least an hour before moving it.
Trimming and finishing
Once the oval has stiffened firmly, invert it carefully onto the banding wheel. Trim the base using the loop tool to remove excess clay and produce a tidy
foot-ring
. The foot-ring on an oval dish should follow the oval outline: trim it freehand following the new shape, not mechanically around a circle. Aim for 6 mm tall and 8 mm wide.
Allow the dish to dry fully to
bone-dry
before
bisque
firing.
Glazing and glaze firing

After bisque firing, wax the foot-ring. Glaze inside and out with the mid-fire glaze. Dip the whole dish into the glaze bucket for the most even coverage, keeping the foot-ring clear. Alternatively, brush with the mop brush in three overlapping coats. Fire to cone 6 (1220 degrees C).
Variations
Wider oval: press the rim inward more aggressively at the two short-axis points to produce a more elongated shape. The walls at the short ends will be slightly higher than at the long sides.
Pinched handles: at the same leather-hard stage as reshaping, attach two small pulled lugs at each short end of the oval for a gratin dish look.

rows.cause: The clay had dried past the leather-hard window before reshaping was attempted
rows.fix: Wrap the piece in a damp cloth for 30 to 60 minutes to re-introduce moisture before the next attempt. If the crack is small, press it closed and smooth with a damp fingertip.

rows.problem: Dish sprang back to round after reshaping
rows.cause: The clay was still too soft and elastic at the time of reshaping
rows.fix: Leave the piece for another hour or two after reshaping, supporting the short sides with small folded pieces of foam or cloth to hold the oval shape while the clay stiffens.

rows.cause: Floor was not compressed after opening, or the dish dried unevenly
rows.fix: Compress the floor immediately after opening with two passes of the wooden rib. Dry slowly under plastic for the first 12 hours.
